schools.summary: Jacob continues his journey to the land of the east: 'then Jacob went on his journey, and came into the land of the people of the east' — the Hebrew ('Jacob lifted up his feet') suggesting that, after the vision and the promise at Bethel (28:12-15), Jacob went forward with renewed cheer and lightness of step (the comfort of the covenant-God speeding the pilgrim on, the encouraged traveler); the lively phrase — Jacob, assured of God's presence (28:15), proceeding with courage and gladness (the comfort of the vision bearing him up, the pilgrim strengthened for the way); 'came into the land of the people of the east' (Jacob reaching the region of Mesopotamia/Padan-aram, the lands east of Canaan, the kindred, 28:2, the providence bringing him to the place of his kindred, the setting for the meeting at the well, vv.2-14) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: Then Jacob went on his journey, and came into the land of the people of the east
Jacob continuing his journey to the land of the east
the encouraged pilgrim, cheered by the vision at Bethel (28:15)
the providence bringing him to the kindred's land (28:2)

schools.summary: Jacob finds the well with the great stone: 'and he looked, and behold a well in the field, and, lo, there were three flocks of sheep lying by it; for out of that well they watered the flocks: and a great stone was upon the well's mouth' — Jacob coming upon a well in the field, with three flocks waiting to be watered (the well a place of meeting, as for Abraham's servant, 24:11, the pastoral scene, the setting for the meeting with Rachel, v.6); 'a great stone was upon the well's mouth' (a heavy stone covering the well, to protect the water, and to keep it from being used until all the flocks gathered, v.8, the stone Jacob would roll away, v.10, the custom of the place); the well, the flocks, and the stone setting the scene for the providential meeting (vv.9-12) — Jacob brought to the very well where Rachel would come (the providence ordering the encounter, as with Rebekah, ch. 24, the setting for the founding of the family) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott.

themes: And he looked, and behold a well in the field, and, lo, there were three flocks of sheep lying by it... and a great stone was upon the well's mouth
Jacob finding the well with the great stone
the pastoral well, a place of meeting (24:11), the heavy stone (v.8)
the setting for the providential meeting with Rachel (vv.9-12)

schools.summary: The custom of watering when all the flocks gathered: 'and thither were all the flocks gathered: and they rolled the stone from the well's mouth, and watered the sheep, and put the stone again upon the well's mouth in his place' — the custom: the shepherds waiting until all the flocks gathered, then together rolling away the heavy stone, watering the sheep, and replacing the stone (the orderly, communal watering, the heavy stone requiring many hands, or much strength, the custom that Jacob would override, v.10); the rolling of the heavy stone — requiring the gathered shepherds (the protection of the well, the communal arrangement); 'put the stone again upon the well's mouth in his place' (the stone replaced after watering, to guard the well, the careful custom, the setting for Jacob's feat, v.10, rolling the stone alone, and the meeting with Rachel) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And thither were all the flocks gathered: and they rolled the stone from the well's mouth, and watered the sheep, and put the stone again upon the well's mouth in his place
the custom of watering when all the flocks gathered
the communal watering, the heavy stone requiring many hands
the custom that Jacob would override alone (v.10)

schools.summary: Jacob inquires of the shepherds: 'and Jacob said unto them, My brethren, whence be ye? And they said, Of Haran are we' — Jacob courteously addressing the shepherds ('My brethren') and asking where they were from, they answering, 'Of Haran' (the providence — Jacob had reached the very place, Haran, the kindred, 28:10, he sought, the courteous inquiry); Jacob's friendly address ('My brethren') — the courteous manner of the patriarch (the polite inquiry, the seeking of information about the place and the kindred); 'Of Haran are we' (the shepherds of Haran, the city of Nahor, the kindred, 24:10; 27:43, the providence bringing Jacob exactly to the right place, the way to learning of Laban, v.5, and meeting Rachel, v.6, the encounter at the well) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And Jacob said unto them, My brethren, whence be ye? And they said, Of Haran are we
Jacob inquiring of the shepherds
the courteous address and inquiry of the patriarch
the providence bringing Jacob exactly to Haran, the kindred's place (24:10; 27:43)

schools.summary: Jacob asks after Laban: 'and he said unto them, Know ye Laban the son of Nahor? And they said, We know him' — Jacob asking after Laban (his uncle, Rebekah's brother, 24:29, 'son of Nahor,' i.e., grandson of Nahor, 22:20-23, or 'son' used loosely for descendant), the shepherds knowing him (the providence bringing Jacob to those who knew his kindred, the way to the meeting); Laban reckoned 'son of Nahor' (descendant, properly son of Bethuel, son of Nahor, 24:24, the kindred of Abraham, the family Jacob sought, 28:2); 'We know him' (the shepherds knowing Laban, the providence confirming Jacob's arrival at the right place, the way to learning of Rachel's coming, v.6, the encounter ordered) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And he said unto them, Know ye Laban the son of Nahor? And they said, We know him
Jacob asking after Laban
Laban the kindred of Abraham (24:29; 22:20-23)
the providence confirming Jacob's arrival at the right place

schools.summary: The news that Laban was well and Rachel was coming: 'and he said unto them, Is he well? And they said, He is well: and, behold, Rachel his daughter cometh with the sheep' — Jacob asking after Laban's welfare (the Hebrew, 'Is there peace to him?'), the shepherds answering that he was well (the courteous inquiry after the kindred's welfare, the friendly news); as Jacob asked, 'behold, Rachel his daughter cometh with the sheep' (at that very moment Rachel, Laban's daughter, the shepherdess, coming with the flock, the providence bringing her at the very time, as Rebekah came as Abraham's servant prayed, 24:15, the timely meeting, the daughter who would be Jacob's beloved wife); Rachel a keeper of her father's sheep (v.9, the providence bringing the future wife at the moment of the inquiry, the meeting at the well, as with Rebekah, ch. 24, the encounter that would found the family) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And he said unto them, Is he well? And they said, He is well: and, behold, Rachel his daughter cometh with the sheep
the news that Laban was well and Rachel was coming
the timely providence bringing Rachel at the very moment (as Rebekah, 24:15)
the future wife coming to the well, the shepherdess (v.9)

schools.summary: Jacob urges the shepherds to water and feed the flocks: 'and he said, Lo, it is yet high day, neither is it time that the cattle should be gathered together: water ye the sheep, and go and feed them' — Jacob observing that it was still broad day (high noon, not evening), not yet time to gather the flocks for the night (the suggestion that they should water and graze the sheep, not idle, the practical observation of the husbandman); Jacob's counsel — to water the sheep and return them to pasture (not waste the day, whether from diligence, or from a wish, it may be, to have Rachel come and the shepherds gone, the practical, industrious suggestion); Jacob's urging the shepherds to water and feed reflecting his industrious spirit (the diligent shepherd, as he would prove with Laban's flocks, chs. 30-31, perhaps also his eagerness regarding Rachel's coming, the practical counsel) — Ellicott, Gill, JFB, Clarke.

themes: And he said, Lo, it is yet high day, neither is it time that the cattle should be gathered together: water ye the sheep, and go and feed them
Jacob urging the shepherds to water and feed the flocks
the practical observation and counsel of the husbandman
Jacob's industrious spirit (the diligent shepherd, chs. 30-31)

schools.summary: The shepherds' explanation of the custom: 'and they said, We cannot, until all the flocks be gathered together, and till they roll the stone from the well's mouth; then we water the sheep' — the shepherds explaining that they could not water until all the flocks were gathered and the heavy stone rolled away (by the joint effort, the custom, v.3, the heavy stone requiring many hands, the orderly, communal arrangement); the shepherds waiting for all the flocks (the custom of watering together, v.3, the orderly sharing of the well, the reason they did not water yet); 'till they roll the stone from the well's mouth' (the heavy stone, v.2, requiring the gathered shepherds to roll it, the labor, the custom that Jacob would override alone, v.10, rolling it single-handed, moved by the sight of Rachel, the contrast that displayed Jacob's strength) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And they said, We cannot, until all the flocks be gathered together, and till they roll the stone from the well's mouth; then we water the sheep
the shepherds' explanation of the custom
the communal watering and the heavy stone (vv.2-3)
the custom that Jacob would override alone (v.10)

schools.summary: Rachel arrives with her father's sheep: 'and while he yet spake with them, Rachel came with her father's sheep: for she kept them' — as Jacob still spoke with the shepherds, Rachel arriving with Laban's flock (the providence bringing her at the very moment, as foretold, v.6, as Rebekah, 24:15, the timely arrival of the future wife, the meeting at the well); Rachel keeping her father's sheep — a shepherdess (the daughter of a wealthy man, yet tending the flock, the simple, industrious life of the patriarchal household, as Rebekah drew water, ch. 24, the honest labor of the maiden, the future wife at her work); 'for she kept them' (Rachel the keeper of the sheep, the industrious maiden, the providence bringing her to the well at the moment Jacob was there, the meeting that would lead to the marriage, vv.10-30) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Wesley.

themes: And while he yet spake with them, Rachel came with her father's sheep: for she kept them
Rachel arriving with her father's sheep
the providential arrival at the very moment (v.6; 24:15)
the industrious shepherdess, the future wife at her work

schools.summary: Jacob rolls away the stone and waters Rachel's flock: 'and it came to pass, when Jacob saw Rachel the daughter of Laban his mother's brother, and the sheep of Laban his mother's brother, that Jacob went near, and rolled the stone from the well's mouth, and watered the flock of Laban his mother's brother' — Jacob, seeing his cousin Rachel (and the flock of his uncle Laban, the threefold 'his mother's brother' stressing the kindred, the moving sight of the kindred, the recognition that prompted his eager action); Jacob 'rolled the stone from the well's mouth' — single-handed (the stone the shepherds rolled only together, v.8, the feat of strength, moved by the sight of Rachel, love and kinship lending vigor, the eager courtesy, the patriarch's strength); 'watered the flock of Laban his mother's brother' (Jacob watering Rachel's, Laban's, sheep, the courteous service to his kindred, the eager helpfulness, the meeting that led to the kiss and the welcome, vv.11-14) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And it came to pass, when Jacob saw Rachel... that Jacob went near, and rolled the stone from the well's mouth, and watered the flock of Laban his mother's brother
Jacob rolling away the stone and watering Rachel's flock
the feat of strength single-handed (the shepherds rolled it only together, v.8)
the eager, courteous service to his kindred, moved by the sight of Rachel

schools.summary: Jacob kisses Rachel and weeps for joy: 'and Jacob kissed Rachel, and lifted up his voice, and wept' — Jacob greeting his cousin Rachel with a kiss (the customary kiss of kindred and greeting in the East, as a relative, not yet a lover, the affectionate greeting of the kinsman, the meeting of the future husband and wife); Jacob 'lifted up his voice, and wept' — wept aloud, for joy and emotion: joy at finding his kindred (the safe arrival, the providence, the answered hope, the overflow of feeling at the meeting, perhaps mingled with the memory of his exile and his mother, Rebekah, Rachel's aunt, the tears of a full heart); Jacob's tears the overflow of a heart full of joy and gratitude — finding the kindred, the providence of God bringing him safely to the right place (the emotion of the lonely pilgrim met by his own, the affectionate, grateful tears) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And Jacob kissed Rachel, and lifted up his voice, and wept
Jacob kissing Rachel and weeping for joy
the kiss of kindred and the tears of joy at finding his own
the overflow of a grateful heart, the providence bringing the pilgrim safely to the kindred

schools.summary: Jacob reveals his kinship, Rachel runs to tell Laban: 'and Jacob told Rachel that he was her father's brother, and that he was Rebekah's son: and she ran and told her father' — Jacob revealing his kinship: he was Laban's 'brother' (kinsman, nephew) and Rebekah's son (Rachel's aunt's son, her cousin, the disclosure of the relation, the explanation of his coming); the relation — Jacob the son of Rebekah (Laban's sister, 24:29), so Laban's nephew (loosely 'brother,' kinsman, the kindred connection that explained the meeting, the cousin revealed); 'she ran and told her father' (Rachel hastening home with the news, as Rebekah had run, 24:28, the eager report, the news that brought Laban out to meet Jacob, v.13, the providence advancing the meeting) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Jacob told Rachel that he was her father's brother, and that he was Rebekah's son: and she ran and told her father
Jacob revealing his kinship and Rachel running to tell Laban
the kindred connection (Rebekah, Laban's sister, 24:29)
the eager report that brought Laban out (as Rebekah, 24:28)

schools.summary: Laban welcomes Jacob into his house: 'and it came to pass, when Laban heard the tidings of Jacob his sister's son, that he ran to meet him, and embraced him, and kissed him, and brought him to his house. And he told Laban all these things' — Laban, hearing of Jacob (his nephew, Rebekah's son), running out, embracing and kissing him, and bringing him home (the hospitable welcome of the kindred, as Laban had welcomed Abraham's servant, 24:29-31, the warm reception); Laban's eager welcome — running, embracing, kissing (the affection of the kindred, though Laban's later conduct, chs. 30-31, would show his covetousness, some noting his eagerness may have been partly an eye to gain, recalling the gifts of ch. 24, the warm reception of the nephew); 'he told Laban all these things' (Jacob recounting to Laban all that had happened, his coming, the reason, perhaps the family matters, the full account, the establishing of the relation and the welcome into the house, the beginning of the sojourn, vv.14-30) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And it came to pass, when Laban heard the tidings of Jacob his sister's son, that he ran to meet him, and embraced him, and kissed him, and brought him to his house
Laban welcoming Jacob into his house
the hospitable reception of the kindred (as 24:29-31)
Jacob's full account, the beginning of the sojourn (vv.14-30)

schools.summary: Laban owns Jacob as kindred: 'and Laban said to him, Surely thou art my bone and my flesh. And he abode with him the space of a month' — Laban acknowledging Jacob as his near kinsman ('bone and flesh,' the same blood, as 2:23; 2 Samuel 5:1, the owning of the relation, the welcome of the nephew as family, the close kinship affirmed); the phrase 'bone and flesh' — the acknowledgment of close blood-relation (Laban owning Jacob as his own kindred, the basis for the welcome and the sojourn); 'he abode with him the space of a month' (Jacob staying a month, perhaps working, making himself useful, before the bargain, v.15, the beginning of the long sojourn, twenty years, 31:38, the setting for the agreement about wages, v.15, and the marriages) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And Laban said to him, Surely thou art my bone and my flesh. And he abode with him the space of a month
Laban owning Jacob as kindred
the acknowledgment of close blood-relation ('bone and flesh,' 2:23)
the beginning of the long sojourn (a month, then twenty years, 31:38)

schools.summary: Laban proposes wages for Jacob's service: 'and Laban said unto Jacob, Because thou art my brother, shouldest thou therefore serve me for nought? tell me, what shall thy wages be?' — Laban, seeing Jacob serve (the month, v.14), proposing to give him wages (not to let a kinsman serve for nothing, the seemingly generous offer, yet, the witnesses note, Laban's words may already betray his eye to gain, the shrewd bargainer, who would prove covetous, chs. 30-31); the offer — that Jacob should not serve for nothing (the appearance of fairness, the setting for the bargain, the seven years for Rachel, v.18, Laban's proposal that opened the way); 'tell me, what shall thy wages be?' (Laban inviting Jacob to name his wages, the occasion for Jacob, who loved Rachel, v.18, to offer seven years' service for her, the bargain that would lead to the deception, vv.21-25, Laban's shrewd opening, he gained seven years' service, and would gain seven more) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Wesley.

themes: And Laban said unto Jacob, Because thou art my brother, shouldest thou therefore serve me for nought? tell me, what shall thy wages be?
Laban proposing wages for Jacob's service
the seemingly generous offer (yet with an eye to gain, chs. 30-31)
the bargain opened, the occasion for the seven years for Rachel (v.18)

schools.summary: Laban's two daughters introduced: 'and Laban had two daughters: the name of the elder was Leah, and the name of the younger was Rachel' — Laban having two daughters: Leah (the elder, whose name may mean 'weary,' or 'wild cow') and Rachel (the younger, 'ewe,' the introduction of the two who would become Jacob's wives, the elder and younger, on whose order the deception would turn, v.26); Leah the elder daughter (the firstborn, v.26) — the one Laban would substitute for Rachel (v.23, the mother of Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, vv.32-35, and so of the royal and priestly tribes, the elder sister); 'the younger was Rachel' (the younger, beautiful, v.17, beloved of Jacob, v.18, the future favored wife, the mother of Joseph and Benjamin, the younger sister, for whom Jacob would serve) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Laban had two daughters: the name of the elder was Leah, and the name of the younger was Rachel
Laban's two daughters introduced
Leah the elder (mother of the royal and priestly tribes) and Rachel the younger (the beloved)
the order on which the deception would turn (v.26)

schools.summary: The contrast of the two sisters: 'Leah was tender eyed; but Rachel was beautiful and well favoured' — Leah's eyes 'tender' (weak, delicate, perhaps dull or lacking lustre, the Hebrew rakkoth, variously taken as weak/dim eyes, or, by some, soft, gentle eyes, in any case Leah lacking the striking beauty of Rachel, the less attractive sister, in the world's eyes, yet the one God would favor with children, v.31); the various senses of 'tender eyed' — weak, dull eyes (the dominant reading), or, less likely, soft, lovely eyes (the contrast with Rachel's full beauty, Leah's lesser comeliness); 'Rachel was beautiful and well favoured' (Rachel beautiful in form and face, the comely sister, beloved of Jacob, v.18, the contrast, Leah plain, Rachel beautiful, that shaped Jacob's preference and the rivalry to come, the beauty that won Jacob's love) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: Leah was tender eyed; but Rachel was beautiful and well favoured
the contrast of the two sisters
Leah tender (weak/dim) eyed, the less comely, yet whom God would favor with children (v.31)
Rachel beautiful and well favoured, whom Jacob loved (v.18)

schools.summary: Jacob's love for Rachel and his offer of seven years' service: 'and Jacob loved Rachel; and said, I will serve thee seven years for Rachel thy younger daughter' — Jacob loving Rachel (the beautiful younger daughter, v.17, the genuine, deep affection of the patriarch, the love that would shape his offer and his long service, the love that made the years light, v.20); Jacob offering 'I will serve thee seven years for Rachel thy younger daughter' — to serve Laban seven years for Rachel (in place of a dowry, which Jacob, a fugitive without goods, could not pay, the labor as the bride-price, the custom where a portion was wanting, the long service offered for love, the bargain, which Laban accepted, v.19, and used to his own gain); 'Rachel thy younger daughter' (Jacob specifying Rachel, the younger, the precise naming that Laban would later exploit, substituting Leah, the elder, vv.23-26, the love fixed on Rachel, the bargain made for her) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Jacob loved Rachel; and said, I will serve thee seven years for Rachel thy younger daughter
Jacob's love for Rachel and his offer of seven years' service
the long service as the bride-price (the portion he could not pay)
the love fixed on the younger daughter, specified (vv.23-26)

schools.summary: Laban accepts Jacob's offer: 'and Laban said, It is better that I give her to thee, than that I should give her to another man: abide with me' — Laban accepting the bargain, saying it was better to give Rachel to Jacob (a kinsman) than to a stranger (the seemingly favorable answer, the agreement to the seven years' service, the consent that bound Jacob to the long labor, and would lead to the deception, vv.23-26); Laban's words — preferring to keep the match in the family (the kinsman over the stranger, yet, the witnesses note, Laban's real motive likely gain, the seven years' service, and the keeping of Jacob, the prosperer, chs. 30-31, the consent with an eye to advantage); 'abide with me' (Laban bidding Jacob stay and serve, the binding of the bargain, the beginning of the long service, the setting for the deception, vv.21-26, and the marriages) — Ellicott, Gill, JFB.

themes: And Laban said, It is better that I give her to thee, than that I should give her to another man: abide with me
Laban accepting Jacob's offer
the consent (with an eye to gain) binding Jacob to the seven years' service
the way to the deception (vv.23-26) and the marriages

schools.summary: The seven years that seemed but a few days for love: 'and Jacob served seven years for Rachel; and they seemed unto him but a few days, for the love he had to her' — Jacob faithfully serving Laban the full seven years (as the bride-price, v.18, the long labor of the patriarch, the diligent service, which Laban profited by, chs. 30-31, the years of faithful work for love); the beautiful word — 'they seemed unto him but a few days, for the love he had to her': the seven years (a long time) seeming short to Jacob because of his love for Rachel (the power of love to make hard and long labor light and brief, the truest picture of love's labor — that affection sweetens toil and shortens time, the patriarch's deep, patient love, 'love makes the longest service short, and the hardest easy,' the proverbial wisdom, some applying it to the believer's love for Christ, which makes service and waiting light, the love that counts the years but a few days); Jacob's seven years' patient service for love displaying the constancy and depth of his affection (the contrast with the world's hasty, shallow loves, the labor of love, 1 Thessalonians 1:3, the patience that love inspires, the years made short by love) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

debates.question: What does 'they seemed but a few days, for the love he had to her' display?

sides.position: The power of love to make long, hard service light and brief - the truest picture of love's labor (love makes the longest service short, the hardest easy), the patriarch's deep, patient love; applied by some to the believer's love for Christ

themes: And Jacob served seven years for Rachel; and they seemed unto him but a few days, for the love he had to her
the seven years that seemed but a few days for love (the crux of love's labor)
the power of love to make long, hard service light and brief
the patriarch's deep, patient love (applied by some to the believer's love for Christ)

schools.summary: Jacob claims his wife after the seven years: 'and Jacob said unto Laban, Give me my wife, for my days are fulfilled, that I may go in unto her' — Jacob, the seven years (v.20) fulfilled, claiming Rachel as his wife (the term of service complete, the patriarch asking for the promised bride, the claim that Laban would answer with the deception, vv.22-25); Jacob's 'days are fulfilled' — the seven years completed (v.20, the term of the bargain, v.18, done, the just claim of the promised wife, the patriarch's right); 'that I may go in unto her' (Jacob seeking the consummation of the marriage, the plain, honest claim, the request that set the stage for Laban's substitution of Leah, vv.22-25, the marriage at hand) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And Jacob said unto Laban, Give me my wife, for my days are fulfilled, that I may go in unto her
Jacob claiming his wife after the seven years
the term of service complete (v.20), the just claim of the promised bride
the claim Laban would answer with the deception (vv.22-25)

schools.summary: The wedding feast Laban made: 'and Laban gathered together all the men of the place, and made a feast' — Laban making a wedding feast, gathering the men of the place (the customary marriage celebration, the wedding feast lasting a week, v.27; Judges 14:10-12, the public solemnity, the occasion under cover of which Laban would substitute Leah, v.23); the gathering of the men — the public wedding feast (the festivity, the cover, the evening, v.23, and perhaps the veiling of the bride and the wine, for the deception); 'made a feast' (the marriage feast, the joy and ceremony, yet the occasion of Laban's fraud, substituting the veiled Leah at evening, v.23, the festivity that masked the deception, the wedding that turned to guile) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Laban gathered together all the men of the place, and made a feast
the wedding feast Laban made
the customary marriage celebration (lasting a week, v.27)
the occasion under cover of which Laban would substitute Leah (v.23)

schools.summary: Laban substitutes Leah by night: 'and it came to pass in the evening, that he took Leah his daughter, and brought her to him; and he went in unto her' — at evening (in the dark, the bride veiled, v.25), Laban bringing Leah (not Rachel) to Jacob (the fraud — the elder substituted for the younger, the deception under cover of night and the veil, Laban's guile); the means of the deception — the darkness, the veiled bride (the Eastern custom of the heavily veiled bride), and perhaps the wine of the feast (v.22, Jacob deceived as to which sister, the fraud accomplished, Leah brought in Rachel's place); 'he went in unto her' (Jacob, deceived, consummating the marriage with Leah, thinking her Rachel, the deception complete, the discovery to come in the morning, v.25, the elder sister married to Jacob, and so the mother of the elder tribes, vv.32-35) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And it came to pass in the evening, that he took Leah his daughter, and brought her to him; and he went in unto her
Laban substituting Leah by night
the fraud - the elder substituted for the younger, under the veil and the dark
the deception that married Leah to the unwitting Jacob (discovered, v.25)

schools.summary: Laban gives Zilpah to Leah: 'and Laban gave unto his daughter Leah Zilpah his maid for an handmaid' — Laban giving Leah a maidservant, Zilpah (as a wedding-gift, the custom of giving a handmaid to the bride, as Rebekah had her nurse and damsels, 24:59, 61, the provision for the bride, Zilpah who would later bear Jacob sons, Gad and Asher, 30:9-13, the handmaid given); Zilpah given to Leah (a maidservant, fewer than Bethuel gave Rebekah, 24:61, the witnesses note, the customary provision, the handmaid who would bear two of the twelve sons, 30:9-13); Zilpah, given to Leah, would (with Bilhah, v.29) become a mother of the tribes (Gad, Asher, 30:9-13, the providence preparing the handmaids whose sons would be numbered among the twelve, the household of Jacob being gathered) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And Laban gave unto his daughter Leah Zilpah his maid for an handmaid
Laban giving Zilpah to Leah
the customary provision of a handmaid for the bride (24:59)
Zilpah the future mother of Gad and Asher (30:9-13)

schools.summary: Jacob discovers the deception (the deceiver deceived): 'and it came to pass, that in the morning, behold, it was Leah: and he said to Laban, What is this thou hast done unto me? did not I serve with thee for Rachel? wherefore then hast thou beguiled me?' — 'in the morning, behold, it was Leah' (when daylight came, Jacob discovering that he had been given Leah, not Rachel, the deception exposed, the shock of the morning, and here the witnesses marking the striking providence — the deceiver Jacob now himself deceived: he who had personated his elder brother Esau to deceive his blind father, ch. 27, getting the blessing by guile, now, in the dark, deceived by the substitution of the elder sister for the younger, the just retribution, God repaying Jacob in his own coin, measure for measure, the supplanter supplanted, the chastening of the deceiver by deception); Jacob's just complaint — he had served for Rachel (vv.18-20), yet Leah was given (the protest against the fraud, the wronged Jacob, yet feeling now what he had made his father and Esau feel, ch. 27); 'wherefore then hast thou beguiled me?' (Jacob charging Laban with beguiling, deceiving, him, the very word that fit his own former deceit, the deceiver crying out against deception, the providence, and Laban's guile, overruled to chasten and humble the patriarch, the bitter lesson) — Calvin, Gill, Wesley.

debates.question: What does Jacob's being deceived display?

sides.position: The deceiver deceived - Jacob, who deceived his blind father by personating the elder Esau (ch. 27), now himself deceived in the dark by the elder Leah substituted for the younger Rachel; the just retribution of God, repaying the supplanter in his own coin (measure for measure)

themes: And it came to pass, that in the morning, behold, it was Leah... wherefore then hast thou beguiled me?
Jacob discovering the deception (the crux of the deceiver deceived)
Jacob, who deceived his blind father by personating the elder (ch. 27), now deceived by the elder sister
the just retribution of God, repaying the supplanter in his own coin (measure for measure)

schools.summary: Laban's excuse - the custom of the firstborn: 'and Laban said, It must not be so done in our country, to give the younger before the firstborn' — Laban excusing his fraud by pleading the custom of the place: not to give the younger daughter in marriage before the elder (firstborn, the plea of custom, true in itself, an Eastern custom, used to cover the deceit, Laban not denying the beguiling, v.25, but justifying it by custom); the custom — to marry the elder daughter before the younger (a known Eastern custom, yet, the witnesses note, Laban should have said so at the first, v.18, the custom pleaded only after the fraud, the excuse covering the guile); Laban's plea of 'the firstborn before the younger' carrying an unintended sting — Jacob, who had set the younger (himself) before the firstborn (Esau), getting the birthright and blessing (25:33; 27), now answered by the very principle (the firstborn's right) he had overturned (the providence in Laban's words, the deceiver met by his own deed) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott.

themes: And Laban said, It must not be so done in our country, to give the younger before the firstborn
Laban's excuse - the custom of the firstborn
the plea of custom (true in itself) used to cover the deceit (pleaded only after the fraud)
the irony - the firstborn's right answering Jacob, who set the younger before the firstborn (25:33; 27)

schools.summary: Laban's offer of Rachel for a second seven years: 'fulfil her week, and we will give thee this also for the service which thou shalt serve with me yet seven other years' — Laban bidding Jacob complete Leah's marriage-week (the wedding feast lasting seven days, Judges 14:12, after the week, Rachel also given, the week of Leah to be honored before the second marriage, the custom of the wedding-week); Laban offering 'this also' (Rachel) after Leah's week — Jacob to have both sisters (the bigamy, two wives in eight days, v.28, the offer that bound Jacob to a second term, the two sisters married, the source of the rivalry, chs. 30, the marriage not to be judged by the later law, Leviticus 18:18, the witnesses note, there being then no positive law against it, yet not justified); 'for the service which thou shalt serve with me yet seven other years' (Laban exacting a second seven years' service for Rachel, the shrewd bargainer gaining fourteen years of Jacob's labor, the covetous Laban, chs. 30-31, profiting by Jacob, the long service for the beloved wife) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Fulfil her week, and we will give thee this also for the service which thou shalt serve with me yet seven other years
Laban's offer of Rachel for a second seven years
Jacob to have both sisters (the bigamy), the source of the rivalry (chs. 30)
the shrewd Laban gaining fourteen years of Jacob's labor

schools.summary: Jacob fulfills the week and receives Rachel: 'and Jacob did so, and fulfilled her week: and he gave him Rachel his daughter to wife also' — Jacob accepting Laban's terms (v.27), completing Leah's marriage-week, and receiving Rachel also (the patriarch yielding to the situation, bound by the fraud, and by his love for Rachel, the two sisters now both his wives, within eight days, the bigamy accomplished); Jacob completing Leah's seven-day wedding feast (v.27) before taking Rachel (the honoring of the week, the second marriage following); 'he gave him Rachel his daughter to wife also' (Rachel given as a second wife, the beloved, v.30, the two sisters married to Jacob, the source of the rivalry and the gathering of the tribes, chs. 30, the bigamy, not justified, yet overruled by God to multiply the seed, the witnesses note) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Jacob did so, and fulfilled her week: and he gave him Rachel his daughter to wife also
Jacob fulfilling the week and receiving Rachel
the two sisters both his wives within eight days (the bigamy)
the source of the rivalry and the gathering of the tribes (chs. 30)

schools.summary: Laban gives Bilhah to Rachel: 'and Laban gave to Rachel his daughter Bilhah his handmaid to be her maid' — Laban giving Rachel a maidservant, Bilhah (as he had given Zilpah to Leah, v.24, the customary wedding-gift of a handmaid, 24:59, Bilhah who would later bear Jacob sons, Dan and Naphtali, 30:3-8, the handmaid given); Bilhah given to Rachel — the maidservant of the beloved wife (the handmaid who would bear two of the twelve sons, 30:3-8, the customary provision); Bilhah, given to Rachel, would (with Zilpah, v.24) become a mother of the tribes (Dan, Naphtali, 30:3-8, the providence gathering the four mothers, Leah, Rachel, Zilpah, Bilhah, whose sons would be the twelve tribes of Israel, the household of Jacob being formed) — Gill.

themes: And Laban gave to Rachel his daughter Bilhah his handmaid to be her maid
Laban giving Bilhah to Rachel
the customary handmaid for the bride (as Zilpah to Leah, v.24)
Bilhah the future mother of Dan and Naphtali (30:3-8), the four mothers gathered

schools.summary: Jacob loves Rachel more and serves a second seven years: 'and he went in also unto Rachel, and he loved also Rachel more than Leah, and served with him yet seven other years' — Jacob taking Rachel also to wife and loving her more than Leah (the preference for the beloved Rachel, the beautiful, v.17, for whom he had served, the favoritism that would breed the rivalry and Leah's grief, vv.31-35, the divided affection, as Isaac and Rebekah's, 25:28); Jacob's loving Rachel more leaving Leah less loved (hated, v.31, i.e., loved less) — the source of Leah's sorrow (the bitter fruit of the bigamy and the deception, the rivalry of the sisters, the unloved Leah whom God would regard, v.31); 'served with him yet seven other years' (Jacob serving the second seven years for Rachel, v.27, the long labor, fourteen years in all, for the two wives, the diligent service under the covetous Laban, chs. 30-31, the patriarch's toil for his family) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And he went in also unto Rachel, and he loved also Rachel more than Leah, and served with him yet seven other years
Jacob loving Rachel more and serving a second seven years
the preference that grieved the less-loved Leah (v.31), the divided affection (25:28)
the fourteen years' service for the two wives

schools.summary: The LORD opens Leah's womb because she was hated (God's regard for the unloved): 'and when the LORD saw that Leah was hated, he opened her womb: but Rachel was barren' — 'the LORD saw that Leah was hated' — 'hated' here meaning loved less (the comparative, as v.30, loved less than Rachel, the Hebrew idiom, cf. Luke 14:26; Malachi 1:3, Leah the unloved, slighted wife, and the LORD 'saw' her affliction, the slighted, sorrowful wife, the eye of God on the despised, God's notice of the unloved); God 'opened her womb' — gave Leah children (while Rachel, the loved, was barren, the tender compassion of God toward the unloved and afflicted — that the despised wife was regarded and comforted by the LORD with the blessing of children, the divine redress of the imbalance, the loved wife barren, the unloved fruitful, the providence that comforted Leah and humbled the favored Rachel, the LORD's care for the slighted, Psalm 113:7-9, the witnesses noting that 'the despised and afflicted of men are regarded by the Lord'); 'but Rachel was barren' (the beloved Rachel childless, for a time, the contrast, the loved barren, the unloved fruitful, displaying God's sovereign, gracious ordering — comforting the afflicted Leah, and, in time, teaching Rachel to seek God, 30:1, 22, the providence balancing the household, the mercy to the unloved) — Calvin, Gill, Wesley, Henry, JFB.

debates.question: What does the LORD's opening Leah's womb 'because she was hated' display?

sides.position: God's regard for the unloved - 'hated' meaning loved less (v.30; Luke 14:26); the LORD seeing and compassionating the slighted Leah and comforting her with children (while the loved Rachel was barren), the tender mercy toward the despised and afflicted (Psalm 113:7-9)

themes: And when the LORD saw that Leah was hated, he opened her womb: but Rachel was barren
the LORD opening Leah's womb because she was hated (the crux of God's regard for the unloved)
'hated' meaning loved less (v.30; Luke 14:26); the LORD seeing and compassionating the slighted Leah
the tender mercy of God toward the despised and afflicted (Psalm 113:7-9)

schools.summary: The birth of Reuben and Leah's hope: 'and Leah conceived, and bare a son, and she called his name Reuben: for she said, Surely the LORD hath looked upon my affliction; now therefore my husband will love me' — Leah bearing her firstborn and naming him Reuben ('see, a son,' or 'the LORD hath looked,' the name commemorating that the LORD had looked upon her affliction, her being unloved, v.31, the acknowledgment of God's notice, the gratitude of the regarded wife); Leah owning 'the LORD hath looked upon my affliction' (God having seen her sorrow, the slighted wife, v.31, the comfort of God's notice, the faith that ascribed the child to the LORD's compassion, the regarded affliction); 'now therefore my husband will love me' (Leah hoping that, having borne a son, Jacob would now love her, the craving of the unloved wife for her husband's affection, the natural desire, yet still looking to the husband's love, the hope that would not be fully met, and would turn, at last, to praising God, v.35) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Leah conceived, and bare a son, and she called his name Reuben: for she said, Surely the LORD hath looked upon my affliction; now therefore my husband will love me
the birth of Reuben and Leah's hope of her husband's love
the LORD's notice of her affliction owned (v.31), the regarded wife
the craving of the unloved for her husband's love (which would turn to praising God, v.35)

schools.summary: The birth of Simeon, the LORD hearing Leah was hated: 'and she conceived again, and bare a son; and said, Because the LORD hath heard that I was hated, he hath therefore given me this son also: and she called his name Simeon' — Leah bearing a second son and naming him Simeon ('hearing,' from the LORD's hearing, the name commemorating that the LORD had heard she was hated, loved less, v.31, the acknowledgment of God's hearing, the comfort of the regarded wife); Leah owning 'the LORD hath heard that I was hated' (God having heard her affliction, the slighted wife, the comfort of God's hearing, as Reuben commemorated his looking, v.32, the faith ascribing the son to the LORD, the despised wife regarded by the God who hears); 'she called his name Simeon (hearing)' (the second son a token of God's hearing, the unloved wife comforted again, yet still, as Reuben, v.32, the longing for her husband's love mingled with the gratitude, the regarded Leah, the God who hears the afflicted, Psalm 34:6) — Gill.

themes: And she conceived again, and bare a son... Because the LORD hath heard that I was hated, he hath therefore given me this son also: and she called his name Simeon
the birth of Simeon, the LORD hearing Leah was hated
Simeon ('hearing'), the token of God's hearing the slighted wife (v.31)
the unloved wife comforted by the God who hears the afflicted (Psalm 34:6)

schools.summary: The birth of Levi and Leah's hope of being joined: 'and she conceived again, and bare a son; and said, Now this time will my husband be joined unto me, because I have born him three sons: therefore was his name called Levi' — Leah bearing a third son and hoping that now, having borne three sons, Jacob would be joined (attached, knit in affection) to her (the continued craving of the unloved wife for her husband's love, the hope renewed with each son, the longing for the marriage-bond of affection); the longing — that Jacob would be 'joined' (the word from which 'Levi' is derived) to her in love (the unloved wife still seeking her husband's affection, the hope, still set on the husband, renewed); 'his name called Levi (joined)' (the third son named Levi, from the hope of being joined to Jacob, the name commemorating the longing, and Levi, the priestly tribe, the line of the priesthood, Exodus 32:26-29, the son named in hope) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And she conceived again, and bare a son... Now this time will my husband be joined unto me, because I have born him three sons: therefore was his name called Levi
the birth of Levi and Leah's hope of being joined to her husband
the continued craving of the unloved wife for Jacob's love
Levi ('joined'), the son named in hope (the priestly tribe)

schools.summary: The birth of Judah and Leah's turning to praise: 'and she conceived again, and bare a son: and she said, Now will I praise the LORD: therefore she called his name Judah; and left bearing' — at the birth of her fourth son, Leah saying, 'Now will I praise the LORD' — a marked turning: with the first three sons, Leah's words still looking to her husband's love (vv.32-34, 'now will my husband love me... be joined to me'), but now, at the fourth, she rising above the craving for human affection to pure praise of God — 'now will I praise the LORD' (the heart at last fixed on God rather than on her husband's love, the lesson that the soul finds rest not in human love but in praising God, the turning from self to praise); Leah calling his name Judah ('praise,' from the resolve to praise the LORD, yehudah, a confession/praise, and the witnesses marking the great significance: Judah the tribe of the kings, David, and the line of the Messiah, springing from him, Matthew 1:2-3; Hebrews 7:14, 'our Lord sprang out of Judah,' the Lion of the tribe of Judah, Revelation 5:5, the providence that gave the royal and Messianic line through Leah's fourth son, named 'praise,' the unloved wife the mother of the line of Christ, 'the Messiah springing from him, which gave occasion of joy and praise'); 'and left bearing' (Leah ceasing, for a time, from bearing, resuming later, 30:17-21, the pause after the four sons, Leah the mother of Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah, a third of the tribes, including the royal and priestly lines) — Clarke, Gill, Calvin.

debates.question: What does Leah's 'Now will I praise the LORD' (Judah) display?

sides.position: The turning of Leah's heart - from craving her husband's love (vv.32-34) to pure praise of God, the heart fixed on God rather than human affection; and Judah ('praise') the tribe of the kings and the line of the Messiah (Hebrews 7:14; Revelation 5:5), the unloved wife the mother of the line of Christ

themes: And she conceived again, and bare a son: and she said, Now will I praise the LORD: therefore she called his name Judah; and left bearing
the birth of Judah and Leah's turning to praise (the crux of 'now will I praise the LORD')
Leah rising from craving her husband's love (vv.32-34) to pure praise of God
Judah ('praise'), the tribe of the kings and the line of the Messiah (Hebrews 7:14; Revelation 5:5)
